attack_summary: A stealthy data-exfiltration attack by an unprivileged agent on UbuntuComputer that relied on local file discovery, staging/archiving with tools like find and tar, and outbound HTTPS/HTTP connectivity through a FortiGate to send collected data off-host.

mitigations.name: Remove execute permission from discovery/staging utilities
mitigations.brief_description: Removed execute permission from attacker-used tools such as wget, tar, and find for the unprivileged agent user to disrupt discovery and packaging workflows.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP GET request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses wget instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

mitigations.name: FortiGate outbound web traffic restriction
mitigations.brief_description: Added a firewall deny rule for HTTP/HTTPS from the Ubuntu host in an attempt to block outbound web exfiltration.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP GET request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses wget instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

mitigations.name: Remove execute permission from file discovery and archival utilities
mitigations.brief_description: Restricted execute access to find and tar to break the attacker’s file discovery and archive creation path.

mitigations.brief_description: Set /usr/src/app/staged to root-owned mode 700 so the unprivileged agent could not create or modify staged data there.

mitigations.brief_description: Configured outbound policy to preserve HTTP/HTTPS and ping, but it still failed to stop the attack in validation.

mitigations.brief_description: Tightened read/traversal permissions in /usr/share to limit broad local file enumeration while preserving connectivity checks.

mitigations.brief_description: Created a targeted deny rule for outbound HTTPS to the suspected C2 IP, but the attack still re-ran successfully.

mitigations.brief_description: Reapplied a narrow permission control making /usr/src/app and /usr/src/app/staged root-owned and non-writable by the agent.

key_observations: Host hardening was consistently more effective than network filtering in this session.
The attacker’s workflow depended on local discovery, staging, and archiving tools; disabling find/tar/curl or blocking write access to staging paths reduced success rates the most.

Several FortiGate-based mitigations preserved LAN ping and HTTP validation but still failed to stop the exfiltration path, indicating the network controls were either too broad, mis-scoped, or not aligned with the actual attack behavior.
Many mitigations were only partial successes, reducing success rates without fully eliminating the attack until the final file-permission hardening steps.
Connectivity regressions appeared in some network-control attempts, showing a recurring tradeoff between blocking attack traffic and preserving legitimate outbound access.
overall_conclusion: The attack was ultimately mitigated most effectively by local host hardening and access-control changes on UbuntuComputer, especially restricting execution of discovery/archiving tools and tightening permissions on staging and sensitive directories. FortiGate network controls were repeatedly attempted but generally failed to stop the attack despite preserving required validation connectivity. The final successful mitigations reduced the attack to a non-functional state while keeping legitimate LAN and internet checks intact.
